segments.s554-g1: همی is an older continuous particle meaning “still/continually.” بود is the past tense of «بودن» (“was/continued to be”). The sequence places the adverbial همی before the verb; شاپور is the subject, and با + (داد و رای) is an instrumental/qualifying phrase (“by/with justice and counsel”).
segments.s554-g2: This is a nominal apposition describing Shapur: بلنداختر is a poetic epithet “high-/eminent star.” و links that epithet to تخت شاهی (“the kingly throne”); به جای is a short prepositional phrase meaning “in place” or “in its rightful position.” The whole noun phrase completes the predication begun in the previous segment.
segments.s554-g3: چو here means “when.” سی سال (rendered simply as سال in the word list) is the time span “thirty years,” and بگذشت is the past of گذشتن “to pass.” بر سر دو ماه is an idiom meaning roughly “in the space of two months” or “within two months” (بر سر + time = within/over the course of).
segments.s554-g4: پراگنده شد is a compound: پراگنده (“scattered”) + شد (become/happen) → “was scattered” / “became dispersed.” فر و اورنگ شاه is the affected pair: فر (divine/royal glory) and اورنگ (here the throne, or worldly dignity) of the king; word order places the affected nouns after the verb phrase.
segments.s554-g5: بفرمود is a classical/polite past meaning “he commanded/ordered” (honorific literary form). تا introduces the subordinate clause expressing the desired/resulting action (“that/so that he went”); رفت is the past of رفتن (“went”), and پیش اورمزد places the action in the presence of Ohrmazd (پیش = before/in the presence of).
segments.s554-g6: بدو گفت is a verb phrase “(to) him he said” (بدو = to him; گفت = said). کای is a vocative “O!”; چون گل is a simile “like a flower,” and اندر فرزد places the addressed person among youths/sons (اندر = in/among; فرزد = youthful son/young noble). Together it begins a direct address comparing the addressee to a flower among youths.
segments.s554-g7: تو is the emphatic/poetic second-person subject “you.” بیدار + باش is an imperative construction “be alert/awake” (adjective before the copular-imperative باش). و links the two imperatives; جهاندار باش = “be a sovereign/ruler (of the world).”
segments.s554-g8: جهاندیدگان (plural) means “the worldly-wise” or experienced people; را is the direct-object marker marking them as the object of the following predicate. خریدار باش is an imperative literally “be a buyer” but used figuratively here—“be one who takes on, values, or engages the worldly-wise” (i.e., patronize or employ them).
segments.s554-g9: نگر is an imperative interjection meaning 'look/mark/consider.' تا here introduces the scope 'until' (poetic: 'so that there is no hope'), followed by a prepositional phrase به شاهی 'to/for kingship.' دادِ ندارد امید is inverted word order common in poetry: normally امید ندارد (has no hope), but here ندارد (does not have) precedes امید (hope). Together it means 'there is no hope for kingship' or 'do not hope for kingship.'
segments.s554-g10: بخوان is an imperative 'read/recite.' روز و شب is a fixed time-phrase 'day and night.' دفتر جمشید is a genitive construction 'the book (or register) of Jamshid' with the possessor following the possessed (typical in Persian poetry). The whole clause commands continuous reading of Jamshid's book.
segments.s554-g11: بجز ... مکن is a prohibition pattern: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not do,' and بجز introduces the sole exception. داد و خوبی are the two things excepted ('justice and goodness'). در جهان places the action 'in the world.' So 'do nothing in the world except justice and goodness.'
segments.s554-g12: This is a pair of imperatives joined by و: پناه کهان باش = 'be the refuge of the world' where کهان (the world) follows پناه (refuge) without an explicit ezāfe (typical in older/poetic Persian). فر مهان is similarly 'the glory/splendour of guests' (فر governed by مهان). باش is the imperative 'be.'
segments.s554-g13: به + noun here marks the topic/measure: به دینار 'with regard to a dinar' and کم ناز is a compound meaning 'show little coquetry/affectation' (i.e. do not act vain for small money). Then و بخشنده باش is a coordinated imperative 'and be generous.'
segments.s554-g14: همان here functions as 'likewise' or 'just so,' introducing a parallel command. دادده is an older/poetic agentive form meaning 'giver' (literally 'given/giver'), so همان دادده باش = 'be likewise a giver (of justice)'; و فرخنده باش = 'and be auspicious/fortunate.' Each verb is the imperative باش 'be.'
segments.s554-g15: مزن is the negative imperative 'do not strike/strike not' or more broadly 'do not make (a noise/attack).' بر + کم‌آزار = 'against the harmless/unobtrusive one.' بانگ بلند is a noun phrase 'a loud cry/clamour' (بانگ 'cry' modified by بلند 'loud'). The clause means 'do not raise a loud cry against the harmless.'
segments.s554-g16: چو خواهی که is a conditional 'if you wish that...' (چو = if/when in poetic usage, خواهی = 'you want/wish'), introducing the desired result. بختت بود یارمند: بختت 'your fortune' + بود here used as 'be' (classical/poetic past-form used in subordinate optative sense) + یارمند 'an ally/helper' — 'that your fate be (or may be) a helper to you.' The clause expresses the condition for luck's support.
segments.s554-g17: Imperative clause: همه پند من سربسر یادگیر = 'learn/remember all my advice thoroughly.' همه precedes the noun to mean 'all (of)', پند من is a possessive noun phrase 'my advice', سربسر is an adverbial 'completely/throughout', and یادگیر is an imperative verb ('learn' / 'commit to memory') addressed to the listener (classical Persian uses this present-stem form as a command).
segments.s554-g18: Comparative subordinate: چنان هم که من دارم از اردشیر = 'just as I have (it) from Ardashir.' چنان ... که introduces the comparison ('as/just so that'), هم is an emphatic/associative particle, که is the subordinate connector, من دارم is present-tense 'I have/possess' (here meaning 'I have learned/received'), and از اردشیر marks the source 'from Ardashir.'
segments.s554-g19: Narrative past + result: بگفت این و رنگ رخش زرد گشت = 'He said this, and the colour of his steed became pale.' بگفت is past third-person 'he said' (the prefix b- is the older form found in epic diction), این points back to the preceding remark, و links clauses, رنگ رخش is a genitive 'the colour of his steed', and زرد گشت is a compound predicate 'became yellow/pale' with گشت as the past/become verb.
segments.s554-g20: Result clause: دل مرد برنا پر از درد گشت = 'The young man's heart became full of pain.' دل (heart) is the subject, مرد برنا is a nominal phrase 'the vigorous/young man' (adjective after noun is common in poetry), پر از means 'full of', درد 'pain/sorrow', and گشت again is the past/copular verb 'became/was filled.'
segments.s554-g21: Rhetorical question with archaic particles: چه سازی همی زین سرای سپنج = 'What tune/skill do you (try to) make from this sponge-house?' چه سازی (literally 'what instrument/tune' or 'what making') asks 'what are you doing/making'; همی here is the classical continuative/particle that accompanies the verb; زین should be read as 'ز این' = 'from this' (an archaic contraction; this precise use is not listed among the provided senses), سرای is the present/imperative/stative sense of 'سرودن/سراییدن' here 'you sing/compose', and سپنج (literally 'sponge') is used metaphorically for the frail/absorbent dwelling (poetic image).
segments.s554-g22: Parallel exclamation: چه نازی به نام و چه نازی به گنج = 'What conceit/vanity about a name, and what conceit about treasure.' چه introduces the exclamation, نازی expresses a sense of coquettishness/vanity (here 'vain pride'), به marks the domain ('in/for'), نام is 'name/reputation', and the second half repeats the structure in parallel with گنج 'treasure.'
segments.s554-g23: Statement about fate with enclitic object: ترا تنگ تابوت بهرست و بس = 'A narrow coffin is (all) for you / will suffice for you.' ترا here is the object clitic spelled out as ت + را (second-person object + object marker) meaning 'for you'; تنگ تابوت is 'narrow coffin' (adjective + noun), بهرست is an archaic/formulaic term here meaning something like 'is for' or 'will be your lot' (this specific sense is not in the provided list), and بس 'enough' reinforces that this is all that awaits the addressee.
segments.s554-g24: Outcome clause: خورد گنج تو ناسزاوار کس = 'Your treasure will be taken/consumed by an unworthy person.' خورد here is an impersonal/predicative verb meaning 'befalls/is taken' (not literal eating) placed before the object for poetic word order; گنج تو is 'your treasure' (noun + possessive), ناسزاوار modifies کس as 'an unworthy person.'
segments.s554-g25: نگیرد is the negative present third-person form (ن + گیرد) of گرفتن 'to take/receive' and here governs یاد (taking/holding a remembrance). The phrase ز تو یاد گرفتن (= 'to take/hold the memory of you') means 'to remember you', so نگیرد ز تو یاد فرزند تو means 'your son does not / will not remember you.' Word order is topical/poetic: verb first, then its oblique object phrase ز تو and the nominative (فرزند تو) as the actor.
segments.s554-g26: نه starts this clause as a coordinating negative 'nor' (linking another negative statement). نزدیک is an adjective 'near' modifying the coordinated nouns خویشان و پیوند, and the final تو is the possessive 'your' applying to both nouns; literally 'nor (are) your relatives and your kin near.' The copula 'are' is omitted in poetic Persian and supplied in translation.
segments.s554-g27: ز introduces the source or manner ('as/from'), so ز میراث دشنام = 'an inheritance of insult.' باشدت is an archaic optative/jussive form (باشدت) meaning 'may it be (to you)/let it be yours.' بهر here functions adverbially ('for/as'). Put together: 'May an inheritance of insult be yours.'
segments.s554-g28: This clause states a wished consequence. همه zهر شد is literally 'everything/ all became poison' (شد = became). پاسخ is the noun 'reply/answer' and پای‌زهر is a compound noun ('bane' / poisonous thing) apposed as a vivid description: 'may every reply become poison — a bane.' The terse poetic order omits auxiliary words a modern sentence would supply.
segments.s554-g29: به یزدان گرای is an imperative/advice-like clause: به + noun + گرا/گرای = 'turn to God' / 'be inclined toward God' (گرای here functions as a verbal/adverbial command). و سخن زو فزای means 'and make/increase your speech about him' (ز = 'about/of', فزای is an archaic imperative/jussive 'increase!'). Together the segment is an exhortation to address God and to multiply speech/devotion concerning Him.
segments.s554-g30: که introduces an explanatory relative/cause clause ('for/that'), and اویست here is an archaic fused form functioning as a copula ('it is he / he is so') rather than the proper name; it links to the predicates روزی‌ده و رهنمای ('giver of sustenance and guide') which are predicative nouns describing God. Note: اویست is archaic/formal; روزی‌ده ('one who gives livelihood') and رهنمای ('guide') are complements.
segments.s554-g31: درود تو is a possessive phrase 'your greeting(s)/salutation(s)' with تو as the possessor; بر گور پیغمبرش is the prepositional complement 'upon the prophet's grave' (بر = 'on/upon', گور = 'grave', پیغمبرش = 'his prophet' with enclitic -ش). The structure is a short benedictory clause: 'May your greeting be upon the prophet's grave.'
segments.s554-g32: که introduces a causal or explanatory clause ('for/that'); صلوات denotes the ritual invocation of blessings (the Arabic/Persian term salawāt). تاجست is an emphatic predicative phrase 'is a crown' (تاج + است fused), and بر منبرش ('upon his pulpit') completes the image: the salutation is metaphorically a crown set on the prophet's pulpit.

sentences.s554-s1: Shapur remained, by justice and counsel, an exalted star and the royal throne in its place.
sentences.s554-s2: When thirty years had passed, within two months the king's farr (divine glory) and throne were scattered.
sentences.s554-s3: He ordered (it), and he went before Ohrmazd; to him he said, “O — like a flower among the youths ...”
sentences.s554-s4: You be watchful and be a ruler of the world; be a buyer (i.e., supporter/employer) of the worldly-wise.
sentences.s554-s5: Mark well: do not expect to become king; read Jamshid's book day and night.
sentences.s554-s6: Do nothing in the world except justice and goodness; be the refuge of the world and the glory of guests.
sentences.s554-s7: Be little-affected about a dinar and be generous; likewise be a giver of justice and be fortunate.
sentences.s554-s8: Do not raise a loud cry against someone harmless, if you wish that fortune be your ally.
sentences.s554-s9: Learn all my advice completely, just as I have it from Ardashir.
sentences.s554-s10: He said this, and the colour of his steed turned pale; the young man's heart grew full of pain.
sentences.s554-s11: What music do you make from this sponge-house? What vanity in a name, and what vanity in treasure!
sentences.s554-s12: A narrow coffin will suffice for you — and your treasure will fall into the hands of an unworthy person.
sentences.s554-s13: Your son will not remember you, nor will your relatives and kin be near.
sentences.s554-s14: Let insult be your inheritance; may every answer be poison — a deadly bane.
sentences.s554-s15: Turn to God and increase your words about Him, for he is the giver of sustenance and the guide.
sentences.s554-s16: May your salutations be upon the prophet's grave, for the invocation of blessings (salawāt) is a crown upon his pulpit.
